text: This special issue opens a new avenue of research on international organizations (IOs) by framing them according to a "life cycle" perspective, thereby moving away from the otherwise conventional static and state-centric visions that are commonplace in International Relations (IR) scholarship. While organizational sociology and firm theory long explored institutions as going through life cycle stages from founding, development, routinization, decline and succession, in the context of global governance, IOs are often understood primarily as consequences of the initial coalitional politics among member-states. We argue that IOs have their own level of agency and life cycle trajectory defined by varying levels of organizational vitality that are determined by internal management practices that respond to changes in member states' preferences triggered by exogenous geopolitical events. Moving beyond the "formation" of an international organization by analyzing the conditions that determine its survival and adaptation, this special issue will analyze the processes through which organizations achieve different types of longevity, and the choices that states have to engage in reform processes within established bodies rather than abolition and replacement. In this context, it will examine under which conditions crises and structural realignments could end up by increasing the longevity of an IO, examining the trade-offs faced by powerful member-states during processes of reform and the legitimacy-building practices implemented by organizational bureaucracies in their search for survival. Finally, it will examine the effects of treaty withdrawal to the global governance system as a whole and on individual IOs, and the role of leadership rhetoric that shapes the perceived vitality and/or morbidity of international organizations. By focusing on the life cycle of an IO as the principal focus of the analysis and introducing a temporal dimension to the study of these organizations and their place in the global system, this new approach to the study of IOs can provide a greater degree of stability, because analyzing them as being dynamic entities with a life cycle that goes beyond their formal creation allows us to think in terms of longer time periods and thus to be less prone to focusing narrowly on short-term effects at the expenses of understanding their broader structural significance.
